data.title: Powell Industries: From Backlog Boom To Growth Bust
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Nova Capital as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Investment Rating – "Hold"

From November 2023 to June 2025, we can see that Powell’s backlog managed to increase from $983 million to $1.4 billion, and it attracted a lot of brand new investors to the stock because of its relative cheapness among industry peers at the time. But now Powell doesn’t look as cheap as it was before the 1,089% rally took place over the 5-year period.

Right now, we see that the demand for Powell’s products doesn’t grow as it used to before, and the forward growth rates are not going to be like in the past 5 years. This setup makes Powell fairly valued at best, and I don’t see the same level of undervaluation as many bullishly minded investors still do. I wouldn’t buy Powell stock today, hence my “Hold” rating.

Business Description

Powell Industries Inc. is a mid-cap company, engaged in custom-engineered equipment and systems design and production through fixed-price contracts, and according to their recent 10-Q filing, they serve the oil and gas and petrochemical markets (49.54% of total sales combined), the electric utility market (26.17%), and commercial and other industrial markets (17.3%). The product range they sell is quite broad: Powell manufactures traditional and arc-resistant distribution switchgear and control gear, as well as medium-voltage circuit breakers, and also “monitoring and control communications systems, motor control centers, switches and bus duct systems”.

It’s mainly a US-based business, but they also operate in Canada with about 15% of sales coming from there, and they have some activities in the UK and rest of the world, although it’s not that significant compared to the former geographical areas of their interest.

Powell's Future Doesn't Match Its Recent Successful Past

I’ve already noted above that Powell’s backlog increased dramatically over the past 5 years, but the majority of this momentum in orders came in 2023, when their backlog went up from $592 million as of 2022 immediately to about $1.2 billion (+118% YoY). And since 2023, the growth from that $1.2 billion was only about 8.9%, so in other words, the backlog growth plateaued, and it’s important to note that the amount of orders started to crater. We can see that from 2023 to the fiscal Q3 2025 period, orders went down from almost $1.4 billion to $880 million, so it looks very unlikely that Powell will be able to show the same revenue growth CAGR of over 23% that it had for the past 5 years.

We’re already seeing the first outcomes that Powell’s business momentum is slowing down dramatically. In their Q3, we see consolidated revenues of $286.3 million, which is down by 0.65% YoY, and while the gross profit margin improved to 30.7% from 28.36% last year, and it helped Powell report a 4.4% YoY growth in adjusted diluted EPS for Q3, the room for further margin expansion seems to be capped now. The reason is that based on the Q3 earning call, about 150 basis points of the marginality gain “came from project closeouts and one-time items”, so it was not from Powell’s underlying productivity or pricing power. So, I’d expect that over the next few quarters we could see a gradual comeback to the high 20s percentages in GP margins. The management’s statement on the earnings call seems to align with my findings.

we anticipate that as we close fiscal 2025 and establish our framework for fiscal 2026, margin levels should approximate the current year-to-date margin rate, excluding any unusual items and project closeout gains which together comprise roughly 150 basis points on a year-to-date basis.

And when we add the projected revenue growth deceleration to this equation, I start to see a serious potential problem for Powell meeting the consensus estimates for its forward EPS. Over the past 60 days, the FY2026 EPS estimate from the analysts covering the stock decreased from $14.88 to $13.92, so now it suggests that Powell’s EPS should grow by 17% YoY. It still looks too much on a relative basis, and it leaves a lot of room for further negative earnings revisions in the coming months, in my opinion.

Even if Powell manages to reach that 17% YoY growth in EPS somehow – based on its own 10-Q, about 65% of the current backlog should convert to revenue over the next 12 months, effectively meaning calendar Q2 2026 as the final date. Given the trends in new orders, and the plateau in backlog itself in recent months, I think this conversion leaves little left for the second half fiscal 2026 and full fiscal 2027.

The End Market’s Projected Growth Doesn’t Support A Comeback

First off all, we should understand what drove up Powell’s backlog back in 2023. From my understanding, it was a result of post-COVID industrial and utility customers resuming their capital spending after all the disruptions seen in 2019-2020, so it basically led to a wave of deferred projects releases. Also, Powell’s oil & gas selling market, especially the liquid natural gas (LNG) part of it, saw a massive rebound in new US-based investments in the sector. With LNG export capacity expanding during that period, Powell secured a lot of new contracts and widened its backlog, which led to a revenue surge in 2021-2024.

But when Powell’s backlog was on a rise, and the market expected more revenue boosts going forward, the management itself was admitting that it wasn’t going to last long. At least not in the industrial end market. In Powell's Q1 FY2023 earnings transcript, management states:

And again, on the industrial front, these things have a life. There's a lot of people lining up.

I don't think they're all going to get through over the next, three to five years, but there's, there's a lot of momentum built. And we're, we're thinking the things there.

The industrial – on the utility side, I think, I think the jump in the revenue, this past quarter is really looking back in time out of COVID, sort of the return of a nice cadence to the business and the process that we've built over the last decade. So that's sort of that methodic return to the utility piece.

It turned out in calendar 2025, that once the mega projects completed or started to near completion, Powell began facing tougher year-over-year comparisons. And with Powell’s book-to-bill ratio falling closer to 1.0x again, the new orders are now roughly matching revenue recognition. The cyclicality of the end markets started to work in the opposite direction, and it’s not boding well for Powell’s continued business momentum that many investors are still hoping for.

It doesn't mean that Powell’s end market are not going to expand from today’s values. According to Fortune Business study, electricity transmission and distribution market should expand by a CAGR of 4.06% for the next 5-6 years. And the most important business segment for Powell — the industrial and commercial infrastructure — should have a CAGR of 3.3-8.2% for the next 5-10 years, depending on the specific niche.

But still, what the forward growth in TAM can bring is not sufficient to help Powell show its past 5 years’ business growth rates.

Powell Stock’s Valuation Is Fair At Best

Many of us got used to the fact that tech stocks tend to trade at 30-40x forward earnings, which is explained by their sustainably high margins and business growth rates. But it’s usually not the case for companies like Powell, because as I’ve already mentioned today, the firm’s margins are not going to stay where they are now. They’re very likely to drop, and even the management sees and guides for a similar future tendency.

Right now, Powell stock trades at a forward P/E multiple of 21x, and while it might look cheap to some of you, I think we should take a broader context and see the stock’s historical ratios. It looks as Powell traded for 20x and even 30x before (for example, in 2013, 2016, or 2020), but those periods were then followed by sharp multiple increases like in 2021, or by negative P/Es like in 2016 and 2018.

Now, given that the current signs suggest a nearing end of Powell Industries' bullish operational cycle, the same thing can follow again, in my opinion, and it’s not promising anything good for the current shareholders.

Of course, if Powell’s operations don’t get disrupted the way we saw in 2017/2018 or 2021 financials, and the firm’s margins don’t fall too deeply from today’s levels, then the current P/E may be viewed as a fair one. But it’s the best case scenario for Powell, if my earlier findings are spot on.

Let's assume that the current forward EPS consensus estimates for Powell are correct. Based on quarterly EPS beats, the general trend is downward sloping, and it would be unreasonable to assume the Powell can beat in its 2021-2024 manner over the next few years. So, again, assuming that Powell's earnings per share of $13.52 for fiscal 2026 is the closest-to-reality input, the implied P/E multiple for the subsequent year arrives at 22.41, according to Seeking Alpha.

As I mentioned above, today's multiple is what Powell would deserve to trade for, given the general stability of its underlying business. In this case, today's 21x earnings is what we can apply for the fiscal 2026 EPS figure. By doing that, I get a price target of $283.92, which is a downside of 6-7% to the current price.

Also, even the most bullish Wall Street analysts agree with my price target of ~$280 per share, based on Seeking Alpha's data:

Conclusion

I can’t say that Powell Industries is a poorly managed company, or that it has no prospects for another backlog and revenue boosting. But I think that the current fundamental picture suggests that the next few years will likely mark low relative growth rates, and if so, the stock’s valuation today will give GARP investors little or no upside potential. That’s why I decided to rate Powell stock as a “Hold”

data.title: REM: Mortgage REITs For Income Investors
data.description:
The iShares Mortgage Real Estate ETF (BATS:REM) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund [ETF] designed to provide investors with exposure to residential and commercial mortgage real estate. The strategy charges a moderate expense ratio of 48bps while providing an appealing distribution rate of $1.99/share, yielding 9.05% on a trailing twelve-month basis.

About iShares Mortgage Real Estate ETF

REM was launched on May 1, 2007, by BlackRock Fund Advisors and iShares as a passively managed fund designed to track the FTSE Nareit All Mortgage Capped Index. Mortgage real estate investment trusts [REITs] are a subsector of the real estate market, packaged in a similar, diversified tax-efficient trust.

The ETF has roughly $595mm in assets under management [AUM] with an average of $11mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing investors with substantial liquidity for actively managing a position with a low bid/ask spread of 6bps. REM pays out a robust quarterly distribution rate annualized at $1.99/share, yielding 9.05%. Though the distribution yield is above average for an ETF, Seeking Alpha data suggests that the ETF has faced significant reductions in the distributions over the years. Investors must be aware that this trend could potentially continue, particularly as the underlying real estate market faces maturing debt during a period of higher interest rates.

One of the defining factors REITs have to offer is their tax efficient distributions. In order to qualify as a REIT, the company must distribute at least 90% of taxable income to shareholders, acting as a cash flow pass-through vehicle. One of the benefits to REIT shareholders is that they can deduct 20% of their dividends earned for the qualified business income deduction.

REM was designed to track the FTSE Nareit All Mortgage Capped Index, an index that tracks the residential and commercial mortgage real estate, mortgage finance, and savings associations sectors in the US. Because REM is indexed to the Nareit Index, investors should not expect the strategy to outperform the underlying Index nor deviate from the Index during periods of market uncertainty or stress.

REM should be considered as a highly concentrated strategy with complete exposure to Mortgage REITS. The strategy invests across 31 holdings and exhibits an appealing portfolio valuation of 0.88x price/book value. The strategy has a relatively high 3-year equity beta of 1.51x, potentially suggesting exceptionally high volatility relative to the broader market.

Aside from subsector concentration, REM is heavily concentrated across its top 10 holdings, making up 70% of the total portfolio weight. The top holdings include Annaly Capital Management REIT (NLY) at 19.14%, AGNC Investment REIT (AGNC) at 15.01%, and Starwood Property Trust REIT (STWD) at 9.87%.

Annaly is largely a capital management firm that invests in agency mortgage-backed securities, non-agency residential whole loans, and securitized products, amongst other products. The company has faced some financial headwinds in recent years with tighter net interest margins impacting cash flows. With the key interest rate set for decline, Annaly may be well-positioned to realize improving spreads in the coming quarters.

NLY shares currently pay out a robust distribution rate of $2.80/share, yielding 13.85% on a forward basis.

The second largest holding in REM is AGNC Investment REIT Corporation, accounting for 15.01% of net assets. The company has a similar operating structure to Annaly in which the organization invests in pass-through securities and collateralized mortgage obligations [CMOs]. Similar to Annaly, the organization has faced some headwinds in recent years with a moderate recovery in recent quarters.

AGNC pays out a robust distribution rate of $1.44/share with a forward yield of 14.71%. The strategy has paid out relatively consistent monthly distributions despite cash flow challenges in recent years.

Who Should Consider REM as an Investment Strategy

REM can be utilized for a variety of investment strategies, particularly for long-term income investors. REM has historically paid out a robust distribution rate that can provide investors with current income. REM can also be used for tactical investors seeking to invest indirectly in mortgage-related products. Macro traders can also utilize REM given the sensitivity to the broader real estate market as it relates to economic data and interest rate policy. Advisors can utilize REM for strategic alternative investment positioning as part of a broader portfolio strategy. Though iShares does not have current distribution data available, previous distributions have primarily been taxed as ordinary income with some return of capital; given the historical dividend information, investors may consider REM for a tax-advantaged portfolio like an IRA.

Risks Related to REM

REM is a passively managed investment strategy, imposing on investors certain risks that should be understood prior to making a final investment decision. Because REM is an indexed strategy, investors will be responsible for active positioning as the portfolio will not take defensive positioning during periods of market stress. REM is a concentrated strategy in mortgage REITs, exposing investors to significant concentration risk; REM can best be used as a portfolio diversifier as part of a broader, diversified portfolio strategy.

Distributions have historically varied from period to period, creating some uncertainty for income investors. The real estate market, particularly for mortgage investment products, can be sensitive to macroeconomic trends, particularly as it relates to inflation, interest rates, employment, and other factors that may impact the serviceability and rates of mortgages.

Final Thoughts

REM can be utilized by income and tactical investors seeking exposure to mortgage REITs. REM should be used as a portfolio diversifier as an alternative asset strategy. From an invertibility perspective, investors must weigh the health of the market and whether the underlying holdings can continue to perform.

This article answers these three main questions about REM:

  • What type of investor does REM appeal to?
  • Are there specific risks investors face with REM?
  • Is REM a good portfolio diversifier?

Editor's Note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for education purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Fulton Financial Corporation: Finally An Upgrade
data.description:
One institution that has been disappointing to me over the last several months has been Fulton Financial Corporation (NASDAQ:FULT). Back in February of this year, I decided to reaffirm it as a "Hold" candidate. This was based on my view that, even though the firm had experienced significant growth on the balance sheet, I was worried about changes in interest rates and how the stock was valued. The firm wasn't exactly expensive, but it wasn't cheap either. And asset quality, while not bad by any means, was not quite good enough to get me excited. Even so, the company has experienced some tough times since then, even as revenue and profits increase. Shares are down 6.8% since the time that article was published, far worse than the 9.6% rise the S&P 500 has enjoyed.

Looking at the data today, I see that asset quality has picked up. Shares are cheaper now than they were as well. The income statement is doing fine, and deposits have experienced a bit of growth. Given this combination of factors, especially when combined with the decline in share price that we experienced, I believe that it's finally appropriate to upgrade the institution to a soft "Buy."

Taking a fresh look at Fulton Financial Corporation

Typically, when I analyze a bank or other financial institution, I like to focus on the balance sheet first. During the most recent quarter, which would be the second quarter of the 2025 fiscal year, the company had deposits on its books of $26.14 billion. That compares to the $26.13 billion it ended 2024 with. That's not significant growth by any means. But any sort of growth is better than a decline. Things would have been even more impressive had it not been for the fact that the firm saw a net cash outflow of brokered deposits in the first half of this year of $58.7 million. This brings total brokered deposits down to $817.1 million, or 3.1% of its total deposit base. This is absolutely a manageable amount of deposits. But to see total deposits increase at a time when broker deposits declined is a positive thing in and of itself.

Another thing that we should focus on would be uninsured deposit exposure. I typically like to see a reading here of 30% or lower. But according to management, in the most recent quarter, uninsured deposits amounted to $9.2 billion. That's 35.2% of total deposits. It's not drastically higher than my threshold, but it's high enough to warrant attention moving forward. The good news is that this exposure is actually down from the $9.4 billion, or 36%, of total deposits that were brokered at the end of last year.

The value of loans for the company has declined slightly, dropping from $23.67 billion at the end of last year to $23.64 billion in the second quarter of this year. I would argue that Fulton Financial Corporation is quite diverse when it comes to these types of assets. For instance, its greatest exposure would be the $9.68 billion in real estate commercial loans that it has outstanding. But it also has $6.51 billion in residential mortgage real estate loans, and $4.54 billion of commercial and industrial loans. Whenever I look at a bank with a large amount of real estate commercial loans on its books, I always like to see, if possible, how much of the exposure involves office assets. After all, the United States is seeing high and growing vacancy rates at this time. But the good news, as the image below illustrates, is that only around 3% of the company’s entire loan portfolio involves office assets. And with a weighted average loan-to-value ratio of 63%, there is a good amount of wiggle room here.

Other aspects of the balance sheet should not be ignored. The most recent data that we have indicates that the value of securities amounted to $5.09 billion in the second quarter of this year. That's up nicely from the $4.81 billion the company ended last year with. Cash and cash equivalents, however, went from $1.20 billion to $804.7 million. At the same time, the value of debt on its books remained mostly flat, dipping from $1.78 billion to $1.77 billion.

The income statement for the institution has shown some improvements lately. As the balance sheet of the company grew, and as its net interest margin expanded from 3.43% in the second quarter of 2024 to 3.47% at the same time this year, net interest income increased from $209.7 million to $246.3 million. One thing that certainly helped here was a big decline in its provision for credit losses from $32.1 million to $8.6 million. And for the first half of this year, net interest income of $483.6 million comfortably exceeded the $405.7 million, with a rise in net interest margin and a decline in the provision for credit losses from $43 million to $22.5 million helping out.

Non-interest income, on the other hand, has experienced some weakness. Because of $20.3 million of investment security losses, and the fact that, in the second quarter of last year, the company booked a $47.4 million gain on a business acquisition, non-interest income has dropped from $93 million to $69.1 million. And for the first half of this year, the decline was from $150.1 million to $136.4 million. However, when we ignore certain one-time events like I described already, we do see some areas of strength. For the first half of this year, for instance, wealth management revenue grew from $41.1 million to $44.1 million. Commercial banking revenue popped from $40.2 million to $44.8 million. And consumer banking revenue grew from $26.3 million to $27.6 million. These are all positive developments in and of themselves.

Net income for the institution has been growing as well. Even though the company booked the large acquisition gain last year, for the first half of this year, net profits of $187.1 million exceeded the $151.8 million reported the previous year. And for the second quarter on its own, the company booked a net profit of $96.6 million compared to the $92.4 million reported last year.

If we assume that this level of profitability continues, we can see in the chart above that the company would be trading at a price-to-earnings multiple on a forward basis of 9.9. That puts it just below the 10 maximum that I like to see in the space. It also puts the company cheaper than any of the five institutions that I compared it to in that chart. In the chart below, meanwhile, I stacked it up against five other similar firms on a price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book basis. On a price-to-book basis, three of the five institutions were cheaper than it is. But on a price-to-tangible-book basis, only one of them was cheaper.

I do think that asset quality is another important item to focus on. And honestly, that is the item that shifted this from being a "Hold" candidate to being a "Buy" candidate in my book. In the first chart below, you can see that the company has a return on assets of 1.25%. That's above the 1% minimum that I like to see. It also puts the business above four of the five institutions that I compared it to. And in the chart below that, you can see the same type of analysis using return on equity. With a reading of 12.46%, which is above the 10% minimum I like to see, Fulton Financial Corporation is above four of the five competitors I stacked it up against.

Even though I am turning bullish on the company, I would be lying if I said that there aren't certain risks involved. Primarily, I'm talking about the composition of its loan portfolio. 42% of its loans are variable in nature. And I fully expect that the Federal Reserve will cut interest rates faster than they have indicated through their dot plot. As I've detailed in the previous articles such as this one and this one, I believe that the U.S. economy is heading toward a recession. And if I'm correct, the firm will not be able to benefit all that much from the fact that deposit interest that it has to pay out adjusts slower than interest rate declines happen to be. At least, that is the case when it comes to the variable loans. We also need to keep in mind that 24% of its loan portfolio is adjustable. So we end up with only 34% that is fixed. The good news is that management does have strategies to address this. This includes having $1.95 billion of interest rate call options and $2.45 billion of interest rate swaps on its books. That could certainly help.

Takeaway

All things considered, I believe that Fulton Financial Corporation is looking better now than it did when I last wrote about it. It certainly helps that the share price has declined. When you add that to the fact that it makes the business more attractive and that fundamentals have gotten better, I believe that upgrading it to a soft "Buy" is the best choice at this time.

data.title: Palo Alto Networks: Fade Any CyberArk Rally
data.description:
Palo Alto Networks Inc. (NASDAQ:PANW) is back to strong resistance at the $205 level despite quarterly earnings not matching the associated price tag. The cybersecurity market offers a lot of strong growth possibilities due to AI, but Palo Alto hasn't effectively transitioned the business towards the platformization concept to warrant the premium valuation. My investment thesis remains Bearish on the stock, though the momentum based stock market could run wild with the extra CyberArk Software Ltd. (CYBR) revenues after the merger.

Another Blah Quarter

Palo Alto Networks reported FQ4 '25 results last month with revenues growing just 16% YoY. The company famously moved to the platformization concept over a year ago, yet business hasn't picked back up to a great extent.

The business quickly dipped from the 20%+ growth rates to a low of 12% in FQ4 '24. The big push into platformization came with Palo Alto Networks providing incentives to customers for transitioning to their services, especially considering the complexity of moving a large volume of business to a new platform.

The cybersecurity leader saw platformizations reach ~1,400 in FQ4, up from ~1,000 last year. Remember, these are the numbers of platforms utilized with any individual customer accounting for up to 3 platforms.

Palo Alto Networks has now reached the scale that makes generating fast growth more difficult. As the company acknowledged on the FQ4 earnings call, the cybersecurity business is the first to top the $10 billion level while platformization are booming, yet revenue growth targets are tepid.

The record-breaking number of platformization deals this quarter demonstrates that customers are not just buying products, they are buying into a strategic partnership. We believe that integrated best-of-breed platforms deliver superior security outcomes and our customers are validating this conviction by making larger, more strategic commitment with us than ever before.

The company recently made the deal to acquire CyberArk due to having a major hole in the platformization business, which again questions the whole best-of-breed platform concept. Palo Alto Networks had to spend $25 billion on the identity management business in order to attempt to offer a full suite of cybersecurity services due in a big part to the stand alone product leading the market and providing vastly better returns for investors over the last 3 years.

Rich Valuation Again

The stock has seen the market cap soar to $148 billion based on 713 million diluted shares outstanding for FY26. As mentioned above, Palo Alto Networks just crossed a revenue run rate of $10 billion with a FY26 revenue target of ~$10.5 billion.

The CyberArk deal will alter a lot of the financials with the company guiding to 2025 sales of $1.3 billion. Palo Alto is paying nearly 19x this revenue stream at a premium to the current Palo Alto multiple of ~14x.

Our view is bearish on the stock, but one needs to keep in mind that the CyberArk deal will juice the reported growth rates once the deal closes. If the deal closes in FQ3 '26 (predicted H2 FY26), Palo Alto networks will start reporting another $350+ million per quarter on top of the forecasted $2.6 billion revenue base. In essence, the current forecasted FY26 growth rate of 14% jumps to a reported rate approaching 30%.

Naturally, sophisticated investors understand the pro-forma revenue growth is much closer to the Palo Alto Networks growth rate of 14%. CyberArk forecasts slightly faster growth rates at around 19%, but the small scale of the business will restrict how much the combined business is growing compared to the prior period.

The market is run by algos looking at headlines, so Palo Alto Networks could very well get a boost from the positive headlines while the real benefits from the CyberArk deal is muted. At the end of the day, the stock is expensive at 14x sales. Any gains just make Palo Alto Networks even more expensive while the need to acquire CyberArk questions the whole platformization concept and suggests investments in the independent players focused on specific cybersecurity products are more appealing than the conglomerate in the space.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Palo Alto Networks has only gotten more expensive since announcing the CyberArk deal. The stock market keeps convincing itself the platformization business is leading to better results while the numbers aren't as supportive.

Palo Alto Networks is a strong company, but the CyberArk deal highlights the platform problems. The stock could end up making a strong run above the strong resistance around $205, but investors should quickly fade any rallies.

data.title: Euro Steadies As U.S. Shutdown Weighs On Dollar
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

​The euro (EUR:USD) held modest gains against the U.S. dollar on Thursday, trading near 1.1735 as investors weighed the economic fallout of the ongoing government shutdown. The lapse in funding has suspended key data releases including weekly jobless claims and the September payrolls report, leaving the Federal Reserve without timely labor indicators ahead of its October meeting.

With this blackout complicating policy assessment, Fed funds futures fully price in a 25 basis-point cut later this month. Political friction added to the dollar’s pressure after the administration froze $26 billion in funding for Democratic-leaning states.

EUR/USD price dynamics (Source:TradingView)

Meanwhile, the Supreme Court’s confirmation that it will hear arguments on Fed Governor Lisa Cook’s removal in January temporarily eased concerns about central bank independence, though credibility risks linger.

ECB policy steadies the euro

By contrast, the European Central Bank has kept a balanced stance. President Christine Lagarde reiterated that inflation risks remain contained, giving little signal of further near-term easing. The relative divergence with the Federal Reserve’s expected policy shift has given the euro a degree of resilience, even as the single currency remains in a broader consolidation.

Technical indicators highlight that EUR/USD is holding above the 1.1715–1.1730 support cluster, reinforced by the 100-day EMA. Immediate resistance is layered at 1.1740–1.1745, aligned with the 20- and 50-day EMAs. A decisive close above 1.1790 would strengthen momentum toward 1.1850, while failure to hold 1.1715 could expose 1.1650. The RSI sits near 51, reflecting balanced momentum without signs of overextension.

Outlook

The near-term path for EUR/USD depends heavily on Washington’s fiscal standoff. A prolonged shutdown would not only weaken sentiment but also delay critical data needed by the Fed. If gridlock persists, the dollar is likely to remain under pressure, keeping the euro supported.

Previously, we discussed the euro’s ability to benefit from relative policy divergence, with the ECB signaling stability while U.S. risks undermined the dollar. That narrative remains intact, with the 1.1790 barrier serving as the pivot for confirming renewed upside momentum.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Beyond Gold: You Need To Look At Silver And Miners
data.description:
Silver and miners have outpaced gold YTD

Gold investors are enjoying a terrific time as gold prices kept pushing new highs. To wit, the SPDR® Gold Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:GLD) ETF provided a price change of 47.94% thus far this year as seen in the chart below, far outpacing the broader market’s (represented by the SPDR® S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY)) already-solid return of 14.39%. Looking ahead, leading institutions (e.g., Goldman Sachs) continue seeing further upward potential for gold in the next 1 to 2 years.

As long-term investors in gold, we certainly see good reasons behind such optimism. We continue to hold a sizable exposure to gold as we see an attractive setup, what we call a "golden triangle," that provides supportive fundamentals to gold. More specifically, the three sides of the triangle include geopolitical uncertainty, inflation concerns, and expectations of lower interest rates.

As exciting as the outlook for gold, we also want to bring your attention to two related assets: silver and miners. As also seen from the chart below, as of YTD, the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) and the iShares Silver Trust (NYSEARCA:SLV) have both outperformed gold by wide margins. To wit, GDX achieved a remarkable 129.8% return and SLV delivered 65.17%. Despite their sharp rallies, we keep seeing strong fundamentals that can support continued momentum for silver and miners for reasons to be detailed in the rest of this article.

Mint ratio is still near record high

For silver, I’d like to start with the so-called gold-silver trade. In case you are new to it, it is a strategy in which you sell a certain amount of gold to buy silver when the silver price appears more attractive relative to the gold price, and vice versa. The key metric we use to evaluate which metal's price is more attractive is the so-called mint ratio, which is simply the gold-silver price ratio (“GSPR”). With this background, the chart below displays the mint ratio since the 1980s. As seen, the ratio has mostly been range-bound between approximately a bottom of ~35 and a peak of ~95 the majority of time in the long run. The long-term arithmetic average is approximately ~65 (65.27x to be precise).

The mint ratio was hovering around 88x as of this writing as seen, still far above the historical mean and among the highest levels observed in the past 4 decades or so, indicating an attractive set up to trade gold for silver. The attractiveness becomes even more pronounced when you consider the so-called geometric average (aka, the logarithmic average). Mathematically, the arithmetic mean begins to lose meaning for data that fluctuates more and more widely. The reason is simple – the arithmetic mean becomes increasingly dominated by a few data points towards the upper bound. The historical fluctuation in the mind ratio is more than three-fold and is sufficient to cause a noticeable bias. As such, we feel it is helpful to consider the geometric mean too. Our calculation of the geometric mean for the mint ratio is approximately 57.6x in the past 40 years, notably lower than the arithmetic mean must mentioned. As such, the current mint ratio of 88x is more than 52% above the geometric mean, indicating an even more attractive silver pricing relative to gold.

Besides the mint ratio, we also believe silver prices have more runaway to catch up with inflation. I assume inflation hedging is a key motivation for many precious metal investors’ interest in these assets. The elevated inflation in recent years certainly played a large role in the price rallies of both gold and silver. But I do not believe silver’s price rally has fully caught up with inflation at all if we expand our horizontal.

For instance, the chart below compares the nominal price of silver, the total M3 money supply, and also their ratio for the past 50 years. As you can see, the M3 money supply (shown by the blue line) has experienced such an increase that it easily dwarfs the increase in silver prices, which are yet to reclaim its past peaks. As a result, the ratio of silver price to the M3 money supply (shown by the red line) is actually toward the lower end of the historical spectrum.

For the above reasons, as to be shown later, our exposure to precious metal is heavily tilted towards silver in our growth portfolio.

Miners’ fundamentals have changed

Now, moving on to miners. A bit of 101 first to investors new to these companies. I refer to them as “miners,” instead of gold miners or silvers miner, to be intentionally vague. The reason is that there is no miner to my knowledge that only mines one metal or the other exclusively. When a miner mine one metal, it inevitably produces the other in significant amount as a byproduct. More often than not, the amount is significant enough to make it difficult to tell which metal is the by product.

The good news is that miners’ fundamentals, whether their focus is gold or silver, are largely governed by the same dynamics. A top force governing theses miners in my mode the lead time for them to expand or develop new mining facilities as they sense favorable pricing environments. The development of new mines could take years or even a decade per the report on gold prices and gold miners. The dynamics for sliver mining and production (again, often intertwined with gold mining and production) are very similar.

In some cases, the period between the discovery and production of gold mines can be years. This means that once miners have exhausted and expanded current mines, there will be a lull, due to the current lack of investment in exploration. This issue points to a perennial problem within the mining sector. The World Gold Council has charted five cycles of bull (rising prices) and bear (declining) markets since the 1970s, but it seems that each time a lean period occurs, spending is slashed to keep afloat, instead of investing in exploration.

The second force that governs miners’ performance is their operating expenses, which can often increase even faster than gold and silver prices. The profits of these miners do not scale with gold and silver prices, but the differential between gold/silver prices and their mining costs. A few years ago (around 2021 and 2023 as seen in the chart below), miners have been pressured by large increases in their operating expenses. Take Newmont (NEM) as an example (which is the second largest holding in GDX), the YOY increase in its quarterly operation expenses was on average ~75% during those years, which far exceeded the increase in gold and/or silver prices during the same period.

Fast forward to now, we begin to observe several signs that suggest the above headwinds are ending. To start, many miners that we’ve examined have exhibited notable decreases in their CAPEX expenditures. Take NEM as an example again, its CAPEX has been in decline since early 2024 both in terms of absolute dollar amount (see the top panel in the next chart) and also as a fraction of its sales (see the bottom panel).

Secondly, I believe their CAPEX investments over the past years have begun to pay dividends and boost their operation’s efficiency. The net results from such muted CAPEX requirements and improved operations, especially when combined with the more favorable pricing environment, are terrific profit margins as you can easily see from the following chart. Let me cite a few numbers. NEM’s current gross profit margin is reported to be around 51%. This is not only considerably better than its historical average of 34% observed in the past 5 years but also represents the best margins in this period.

Agnico Eagle's (AEM) (the largest holding in GDX) current gross profit margin is even stronger at 58.59%, again not only far above its 5-year average of 39% but also represents the peak level in this period. The margin expansion also extends to other profitability metrics (such as EBIT and net profit margins) as we walk further downstream of their income statements.

Other risks and final thoughts

As another positive for precious metals, they have historically shown weak correlation with both equity and bond assets, thus providing excellent diversification at a portfolio level. As an example, GLD has demonstrated the weakest (but still positive) correlation with the broader equity market represented by SPY at 0.08, even lower than bond’s (as represented by the Vanguard Total Bond Market ETF, BND) correlation of 0.26. Silver (as approximated by SLV) and miners also demonstrated quite weak relationships with both equity and bonds.

There are many negatives generic to precious metal ETFs and mining stocks. To stay focused, I will limit myself to the issues that are most relevant to this article. First, potential investors need to be aware that both silver and miners are strongly correlated with gold. For example, the data above show that SLV’s correlation with GLD is 0.79 and with GDX is 0.72. Thus, the gold-silver trade strategy and exposure to miners I mentioned above does not help too much with diversification at a macroscopic level if you already have sizable exposure to gold. Second, miners and silver are far more volatile than traditional assets such as those listed in the table above. Within this group, GDX has exhibited the largest volatility in the past, with an average daily standard deviation of 2.59%, which is about 2.5x compared to gold’s 1.09% and about 2x compared to SPY’s 1.26%. Silver has exhibited the second largest volatility with a daily standard deviation of 1.94% as seen. As aforementioned, the profits of miners scale with the DIFFERENTIAL between gold/silver prices and their mining costs, which makes miners a leveraged bet on the prices of precious metals.

To close, the purpose of this article is to share our thoughts on precious metals. Readers familiar with our investment approach are aware that we have long-term exposure to both gold and silver (through the IAU and SIVR ETF primarily). To be perfectly clear, the purpose of this article is not to make bearish argument on gold. We certainly feel positive about gold’s outlook and have a sizable exposure to it in our accounts (about 9% in our conservative portfolio). We just want to bring other related assets to your attention such as silver and miners. As seen in the screenshot below, for our growth portfolio, our precious metal exposure is heavily tilted toward silver with a 7.2% target allocation, compared to about 1.8% for gold.

data.title: XRP Surges As Derivatives Demand Builds And Spot Inflows Return
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

XRP (XRP-USD) traded near $2.96 on Thursday as the market tried to reclaim momentum following weeks of compression inside a descending setup. Buyers again defended the $2.84 to $2.85 area, where horizontal demand aligns with the 100-day exponential moving average.

XRP price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

That zone has absorbed multiple selloffs since mid-August and remains a primary line of defense. Momentum has begun to stabilize. The Relative Strength Index rose to 53 and crossed above its signal, pointing to improving short-term strength without overbought conditions.

The 20-day EMA at $2.91 flipped back into play as intraday support, and price is now testing the upper boundary of the pattern. A daily close above $3.00 to $3.05 would be the first constructive step toward reversing the broader downtrend and would shift focus to higher resistance zones.

On the topside, successive ceilings sit at $3.05 to $3.10, followed by $3.25 and $3.45. A stronger run could put $3.60 on the radar if momentum accelerates. On the downside, a return to $2.84 to $2.85 would test the durability of the base. A decisive failure there would expose $2.62 at the 200-day EMA.

Futures and options show renewed appetite

Derivatives positioning strengthened alongside price. Aggregate futures trading volume jumped 35 percent to $6.7 billion. Open interest increased nearly 6 percent to $7.87 billion, a sign that fresh capital is entering the market rather than only rotating within existing positions.

Options activity surged as well. Total options volume rose 52 percent, while options open interest fell 36 percent. The combination implies that traders added near-term exposure but trimmed hedges, a setup that can magnify follow-through if price clears nearby resistance. Long and short ratios on major venues tilted bullish. Binance accounts showed a 3.28 ratio in favor of longs, while top trader positioning skewed above 2.6. Liquidation data pointed to heavier losses for shorts, suggesting pressure could intensify if price pushes through the $3 handle.

Funding rates stayed slightly positive across major futures venues. The absence of extreme funding reduces the risk of a sudden squeeze in either direction and supports a more orderly advance if spot demand persists.

Spot flows turn positive as accumulation resurfaces

On-chain and exchange flows added to the constructive tone. On Oct. 2, XRP recorded net inflows of $10.38 million, coinciding with the push toward $2.97. The figure contrasts with the outflow pattern that dominated prior weeks and implies that some traders are shifting from defense to accumulation near the established base. One session does not set a lasting trend, but the turn in flows is consistent with improving technical conditions above the 20-day EMA. Market structure remains sensitive to headlines and broad risk appetite. Even so, the alignment of rising futures interest, firmer spot flows, and a defended 100-day EMA gives bulls a clearer path, provided price can establish acceptance above $3.00.

XRP short-term outlook

XRP’s near-term path hinges on the $3.00 to $3.05 zone. A close above that band would confirm a break of descending supply and open room toward $3.25, then $3.45. If momentum extends, $3.60 becomes a stretch target. Failure to clear $3.05 risks a return to $2.84 to $2.85, with a deeper test toward $2.62 if the base gives way.

Previously, we noted that XRP’s triangle compression and persistent exchange outflows kept rallies capped near $3.00. The recent combination of net inflows, stronger futures participation, and a rebound off the 100-day EMA suggests that dynamic is beginning to shift. Bulls still need confirmation through a clean break of resistance, but the balance of forces is more supportive than it was in September.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Taysha Gene Therapies: Shares Are Soaring On FDA Alignment, But Caution Is Advised
data.description:
Investment Overview

Shares in Taysha Gene Therapies (NASDAQ:TSHA) are on the rise today - up >50% in trading so far - after the company reported that:

the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has granted Breakthrough Therapy designation to TSHA-102, an intrathecally delivered AAV9 gene therapy with disease modifying potential, for the treatment of Rett syndrome.

Additionally, the Company announced that it has finalized alignment with the FDA on the REVEAL pivotal trial protocol and statistical analysis plan (SAP) that are intended to support the planned Biologics License Application (BLA) submission for TSHA-102, following the resolution of remaining clinical and statistical queries.

Breakthrough Therapy designation and finalized FDA alignment on the pivotal trial protocol and SAP were based on the FDA’s review of positive clinical evidence from Part A of the REVEAL Phase 1/2 adolescent/adult and pediatric trials.

Investors will be celebrating because the endorsement from the FDA - BTD is designed to "expedite the development and regulatory review of an investigational therapy intended to treat a serious condition"—and its agreement on pivotal study design opens up a clear path to a first approval for a disease-modifying treatment for a devastating disease.

Taysha - Background To Today's News

When I last covered Taysha for Seeking Alpha, it was June 2024, and I awarded the company's stock a "Sell" rating. Management had just shared data from a study of TSHA-102 in two adult patients with stage four Rett Syndrome and two pediatric patients.

I covered the full history of the company since its September 2020 raise of ~$181m at $20 per share, so I will not go over that ground again. Suffice it to say that while Taysha framed the data as positive, noting "improvements across multiple clinical domains," including motor skills, communication/socialisation, autonomic function, and seizures, the market was less impressed, sending shares down in value to $2.9 at the time my note was published.

I had concluded that:

In my view, Taysha management was right to cease development of its other projects and focus on TSHA-102, but I wonder if the data collected to date is compelling enough, given all of the other issues the company faces with funding, single asset risk, competitors, and development path, to keep the share price buoyant in the short-to-medium term, or stave off escalating issues in the long term.

Initially the sell call seemed correct, as shares did fall in value to ~$1.1 in April this year, but stock then began to climb, reaching $3 in late May, before falling slightly, apparently after Taysha shared updates on TSHA-102.

The company said in a press release that it had reached alignment with the FDA around its planned pivotal Part B trial design for TSHA-102, which was:

supported by the Company’s analysis of the International Rett Syndrome Foundation’s (IRSF) longitudinal Rett syndrome natural history study data, as well as clinical data from the ongoing REVEAL Phase 1/2 trials.

Sean P. Nolan, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer of Taysha, is then quoted as follows:

Our rigorous analysis of the robust natural history study dataset demonstrated that after six years of age, the likelihood of achieving defined developmental milestones across the core functional domains of Rett syndrome is highly improbable.

Therefore, it is quite striking that we observed a 100% responder rate following treatment with TSHA-102, with all pediatric, adolescent and adult patients across varying disease severity gaining or regaining one or more developmental milestone that represents activities of daily living that are important to caregivers and clinicians.

According to the Boston Children's Hospital website:

Rett syndrome is a genetic disorder that causes a loss of spoken language and motor skills, as well as behavioral and neurological problems. Children with Rett syndrome may also have a variety of other medical problems, including intestinal, breathing, orthopedic, and heart complications.

In its same May press release, Taysha shared data from Part A of its REVEAL study, this time from 10 females with Rett syndrome aged 6-21 years, 4 receiving a low dose of TSHA-102 and 6 receiving a higher dose.

Besides the fact that 100% of patients showed improvement in at least one defined developmental milestone, Taysha revealed that a total of 22 developmental milestones were achieved across the 10 patients, and that the high-dose cohort achieved a 100% responder rate 25% faster than the low-dose cohort, and that:

Improvements observed across multiple clinician-assessed outcome measures, including Revised Motor Behavior Assessment (R-MBA) and Clinician Global Impression – Improvement (CGI-I), corroborated the developmental milestone gains/regains demonstrated post-TSHA-102

No treatment-related serious adverse events or dose-limiting toxicities were reported by any patients.

Analysis - How Clear Is The Path To Approval For TSHA-102?

In today's press release, Taysha notes that:

Previously aligned upon key design elements for the REVEAL pivotal trial protocol and SAP in support of the planned BLA submission remain unchanged, including:

  • Inclusion of a 6-month interim analysis that may serve as the basis for BLA submission: enabled by the rigorous, objective evaluation criteria used to evaluate developmental milestone achievement in Part A of the REVEAL Phase 1/2 trials and the unprecedented 83% response rate seen at six months following high dose TSHA-102 (5 out of 6 patients; May 2025 data cutoff) in Part A that deepened over time
  • Response rate of 33% (5 out of 15 patients) is the minimum threshold for success sufficient to reject the null hypothesis: based on Taysha’s natural history data analysis, the null hypothesis is that one out of 15 patients aged ≥6 years may gain/regain one of the 28 natural history defined developmental milestone without treatment, corresponding to a response rate of 6.7%

BLA stands for Biologics License Application, and if approved by the FDA—after a 10-month review period, once the BLA application has been accepted by the FDA (in some cases, BLAs are rejected and a "refusal to file" letter sent to the applying company, outlining the reasons why, and inviting companies to resubmit)—then the drug can be marketed and sold commercially.

According to a Taysha presentation from May, the Part B REVEAL study design looks as follows:

The 6-month interim analysis speeds up the BLA submission, and if the responder rates reported from Part A of the study are repeated, then theoretically, the submission would be successful.

Here is an overview of the results from Part A in full:

And here is an overview of the 22 milestones achieved in the study:

In Q2, Taysha made a net loss of $(26.9m), or $(0.09) per share, and reported a cash position of $312.8m - the company completed a follow-on share offering in Q2, raising $230m - which it says will last into 2028, by which time management presumably expects TSHA-102 to have gained approval and been launched commercially.

To answer my own question, then, Taysha's path to approval does appear to be straightforward - in some ways. There are risks to consider - for example, the small study population in the study, and the slightly subjective nature of the developmental milestones, and whether it will therefore be straightforward to reproduce the success of Part A?

Video evidence is required to support the developmental improvements, and was used in the Part A study (so far as I am aware), and on its Q2 earnings call with analysts in August, in response to a question asking when we may see more granular information on the number of video-documented milestones from Part A, Taysha's CEO told analysts:

there's a lot of information that we captured, as I mentioned earlier, and we cast a pretty wide net. And some of that net also included additional videos that we haven't talked about different ways to get at the milestone gain. And so we want to share with the community additional milestones and what those look like, how those translate into activities of daily living. And I think it will give a more fulsome picture of just exactly how the milestones are translating to improvements in activities of daily living and improvements of quality of life of the patients that have been treated thus far.

Personally, I find the study design slightly convoluted, and in some ways am surprised the FDA has signed off on it.

On the one hand, there is a massive unmet need for better Rett therapies - apparently, Taysha's therapy can target a subset of patients with pathogenic MECP2 mutations of ~10k patients - but on the other, is there sufficient evidence that the development milestones are robust enough measures, that the improvements seen are durable, and justify what would likely be the multi-million dollar cost of the therapy, and the stress it brings to patients and their families, given the method of administration?

Concluding Analysis: Would I Back Taysha To Deliver A Successful Commercial Therapy In Rett, & Grow Its Valuation & Share Price?

Gene therapies are complex, and when it comes to analysing their effect on patients from a safety and efficacy perspective, this is also a complex issue.

In 2025, Sarepta Therapeutics' (SRPT) stock has lost >80% of its value as its gene therapy for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy ("DMD") was briefly withdrawn from the market after three patients that had received it died (not necessarily related to treatment).

Often, clinical studies suggest a positive effect, but in a real-world setting, the story may be different. Taysha's track record would concern me slightly - multiple gene therapy programs have been initiated by the company and then shelved, while Japanese pharma giant Astellas apparently walked away from a partnership involving TSHA-102.

In terms of the market opportunity for TSHA-102, analysts have speculated peak revenues of $2.2bn, and I don't disagree that a successful gene therapy for Rett Syndrome would easily achieve "Blockbuster" revenues, meaning that, at its current market cap valuation of $1.29bn (at the time of writing), shares ought to be considered cheap.

For me, however, the data the company has shared to date doesn't feel as compelling as the 100% success rate, and the >20 developmental improvements noted in only 10 patients might suggest.

If I were the FDA, I would not be in a rush for 6-month interim data, but would want to wait longer and measure durability of response, and we may note that besides the study success, TSHA-102 must also be approved on Chemistry, Manufacturing and Control ("CMC") grounds, i.e., how the therapy is manufactured. This can be tricky for a company with a smaller budget, such as Taysha.

As such, in terms of the investment opportunity, I remain firmly on the sidelines. In many ways I hope Taysha succeeds and a new gene therapy be provided for patients - there are not many other therapeutic options in development, other than a gene therapy at the Phase 1/2 study stage being developed by Neurogene (NGNE).

But, I find I am struggling somewhat to find the data collected to date truly compelling, and also wonder what a commercial rollout might look like and whether physicians may have similar concerns - companies such as Bluebird Bio developed successful gene therapies in the past, but ultimately could not successfully sell them in a real-world setting.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Optimism Rises
data.description:
Optimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, neutral sentiment decreased, and pessimism was unchanged.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 1.2 percentage points to 42.9%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the third time in nine weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 1.1 percentage points to 17.9%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 63rd time in 65 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, was unchanged at 39.2%. Bearish sentiment is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 44th time in 46 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 1.2 percentage points to 3.8%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 33rd time in 35 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members if they hold gold or other precious metals in their portfolio, either directly or through an exchange-traded fund (ETF).

Here is how they responded:

  • No, I do not: 55.6%.
  • Yes, I own gold or other precious metals: 18.7%.
  • Yes, I own both precious metals and mining companies: 15.9%.
  • I just own mining companies that focus on precious metals: 7.9%.
  • I am considering adding gold to my portfolio: 1.9%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 42.9%, up 1.2 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 17.9%, down 1.1 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 39.2%, unchanged.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Q2 Holdings: Buy The Dip As Backlog Grows And Margins Expand
data.description:
Underneath the sharp stock market rally that has surprised investors this year, a trend has played out that gets little attention: the very top end of the S&P 500 has been driving all the gains (namely, large-cap tech stocks like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Oracle Corporation (ORCL)), while small and mid-cap stocks are getting sidelined. As I prepare my portfolio for 2026, I'm betting on a rotation the other way as investors seek a return to "growth at a reasonable price" stocks versus "growth at all costs."

Q2 Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:QTWO), in my view, is potentially a great rebound play to check off this box. The banking software company has been a major laggard this year, down ~30% year to date. The question for investors now is, can Q2 stage a comeback?

For me, timing is everything when it comes to investing in a choppy mid-cap growth stock like Q2 Holdings. I downgraded Q2 Holdings to neutral and recommended trimming the position in May, when the stock was trading close to $90. Since then, however, shares of Q2 Holdings have dropped a fresh ~25%, prompting me to take a fresh look at this name. Amid a recent beat-and-raise and strong secular tailwinds for greater automation in the perennially tech-lagging financial services sector, I'm upgrading Q2 back up to a "Buy."

For investors who are newer to Q2 Holdings, here is the long-term bull case for the company:

  • Q2 plays in a large and underpenetrated $23 billion TAM. Q2 Holdings is a "vertical" software company and one of the few software companies that specifically serve a very large niche: banks and financial institutions. The company’s current revenue profile, meanwhile, still represents a relatively low ~3% penetration into its overall market.
  • Land-and-expand business model. The company aims to be a one-stop shop that addresses the complete solutions and pain points relative to financial institutions, and it aims to cross-sell broadly within its customer base. Q2 notes that it has $3 billion of revenue growth potential from within its existing customer base.
  • Firm ARR scale. The company is built on top of nearly pure recurring revenue. Its ARR covers ~90% of its annual revenue guidance, giving the company plenty of revenue visibility and the capability to outperform its outlook.
  • Profit-centric. Despite its double-digit revenue growth, the company is already achieving adjusted EBITDA margins in the mid-20s percentage while also growing FCF at a >2x y/y clip.

Now, Q2 stock also has the benefit of a modest valuation on its side. At current share prices near $67, Q2 trades at a market cap of $4.19 billion. Netting off the $532.1 million of cash and investments against $493.4 million of convertible debt gives us an enterprise value of $4.15 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, Q2 has boosted its full-year revenue outlook to $783-$788 million (12-13% y/y growth), versus an 11-12% growth range previously. Meanwhile, for next year, FY26, Wall Street analysts are expecting Q2 to generate $869.6 million in revenue, or 11% y/y growth. This positions the stock's valuation multiples at:

  • 5.3x EV/FY25 revenue.
  • 4.8x EV/FY26 revenue.

We can now stack up Q2 against other software companies that are currently growing at a low/mid-teens profile, such as Workday, Inc. (WDAY). The stock trades at a ~1-2 turn discount to such names:

In my view, it's a great time to buy the dip on this name.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Q2 Holdings' latest Q2 results in greater detail.

Revenue grew 13% y/y to $195.1 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $193.7 million (+12% y/y) by a one-point margin. We do note that revenue growth did decelerate two points versus 15% y/y growth in Q1, which is one of the core reasons that Q2's share price has been softening since then.

We do find hope, however, in the company's fierce RPO expansion. Total RPO soared 21% y/y to $2.4 billion, representing a sizable amount of contractual backlog that Q2 Holdings has yet to recognize as revenue. The company has attributed this growth to its success in upselling existing customers and driving expansion bookings.

We note that 54% of this RPO, or $1.3 billion, is set to be recognized within the next 24 months. Again, we note that Street revenue expectations for Q2 Holdings in FY26 are only ~$0.9 billion. If we stretch that across two years, Q2's current 24-month backlog already covers a significant portion of two years of revenue run rate, and it doesn't even include any new business that the company can bring in.

On the new sales front, the company notes that it's entering into the second half of FY25 with a strong pipeline. Per CEO Matthew Flake's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

We complemented that with success in the Tier 2 and 3 segments as well, which represented a meaningful portion of our bookings performance. We also had notable M&A activity amongst our customers in the quarter. We had 2 scenarios where an existing customer acquired another financial institution, each resulting in incremental bookings that carried the equivalent of a Tier 1 size deal [...]

As we look to the second half of the year, our pipeline remains solid and our outlook is positive. Even with our performance to date, we expect the majority of enterprise and Tier 1 activity for the year to land in the back half of 2025.

The company also continues to build on its profitability. As shown in the chart below, adjusted EBITDA grew 54% y/y to $45.8 million, which hit a 23.5% margin that improved 620bps y/y.

We note that at the current pace of profit expansion, Q2 may soon be able to justify its valuation from an adjusted EBITDA basis (applying a similar ~24% margin on FY26 revenue estimates yields a forward adjusted EBITDA multiple of only ~20x), which should provide support for its stock going forward. Q2's free cash flow in the first half of FY25 also grew 128% y/y to $79.5 million.

Key takeaways

Q2 is a solid software play with consistent growth results, a healthy backlog, and an expanding margin profile. My previous concern with Q2 was entirely around valuation, which has been more or less solved with the stock's ~30% slide over the past few months. Take advantage of this dip and view it as a buying opportunity.
